speaker: interviewer
text: Leo Berkenwald. This is roll number 4.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how did you feel at the time about the, the treatment you received from the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: As far as I know, and as far as I can judge from what I heard from others who were liberated in different areas, the Russians were excellent to us. Extremely sympathetic. And they showed a sincere interest in helping us. They-- they came-- the doctors came in to us, to examine us, twice a day when I-- when we were still in that German building where the SS were. I'm talking about the first few weeks when we were still in that place. They tried to explain to us why we are treated the way we are treated. Because we were complaining, naturally, that the food that we are getting was not enough. Because the, the amount-- the amount of food that we got every two hours we could eat after being starved for five years. There is no-- there is no amount that I can say-- 10 times as much or five times as much or 1,000 times as much. All we knew is that no matter what we got was not enough at this particular time. And we couldn't wait for the next hour or two to get the next amount of food. And they tried to explain to us in a nice way why they are doing it. Because it is for our welfare not to, not to get too much amount of food, because our stomachs were not capable of absorbing more. And the more we-- the longer we were there, we realized that whatever they are doing, they are doing it for our welfare.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they communicate with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They tried in different ways. They tried to use a few Polish words. They tried to speak to us in German, whoever could. And they also tried to somehow to use the German people like some sort of translators.

speaker: interviewer
text: Interpreters.

speaker: interviewee
text: Interpreters. Without-- I mean, for myself, without realizing, where do they-- where do the Germans come to understand Russian? But somehow, we did not have any difficulties communicating.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after you were moved to this former children's home, how long did you stay there? And what happened after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened after that? I remember we started-- we were allowed to go on, on our own. I don't remember how, but we-- I got hold of, of regular clothing. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I, I would walk out in the streets, and I got clothes through some other inmates. And we were looking for an apartment to go out from there. And the apartments were easy to get because somehow, for some reason, there were enough of empty apartments. And we got, I think, a room or two-- whatever. And we started to, to live on our own. And somehow, this is how-- this is how we-- how we started out.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you support yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no reason or no-- no necessity of supporting because we got the food-- actually, what we needed was food. And we got-- the food came part from that-- still from that children's home. And then there was a Jewish committee formed in, in the town that they-- that they supplied us with food. And then later on, when they-- when stores opened up, and there was a, a way of purchasing the food. We got money from that committee, from that Jewish-- they-- I don't remember the name. They had a self-- self-help-- some sort of a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a cooperative?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like a cooperative. Like a committee. And they, they supplied us with money enough to, to purchase the food--

speaker: interviewer
text: To buy food.

speaker: interviewee
text: --whatever. Yes. And we started to exchange things, and so on, and we started to mingle around with the rest of the population.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned earlier that you never returned to Lodz.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had no desire to?

speaker: interviewee
text: No desire.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I found out from people who went to-- back to Lodz. And they went back to our-- to our houses and to our streets and so on. And they came back. They told us that no one-- no one is alive, and no one came back to look for, for others, that, that they know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it at this time that you found out about your brother's fate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. When you were still in Dornhau?

speaker: interviewee
text: Still in Dornhau, yeah. I don't remember whether it was still in Dornhau or it was later on in Germany that I met this friend of ours.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how long did you stay in Dornhau?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Dornhau I stayed-- I-- if I remember correctly, till about more or less the end of '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. And then what happened after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I-- we decided-- not only myself-- we decided-- because we heard that-- since at that time, Germany was already split in four occupation areas-- that the, the best area for the surviving Jews was the American occupation zone. And everybody longed to get to the-- to the American zone. And in another case, we had to smuggle ourselves twice because our area became a Polish territory. This Polish territory was adjacent to the German-- to the Russian occupation territory.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany. And we had to go from the Polish-- this was part of Poland that was part, originally, Germany that Poland took over. And then we went-- we had to smuggle ourselves out into the German-- into the Russian-occupied territory. And from there, into the American territory.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had to cross two borders.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two borders.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't have any papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. In one, I got some false papers as a-- as a German, as a German coming from, from Russia. And when we stopped on the-- on the Russian-American border, we-- we saw-- we showed the papers to the Russian soldiers, and they didn't know the difference between English and, and, and, and German. They just let us through. To go from the Polish border into the Russian territory, I went to it by train. And I was hiding in a toilet in that-- in the-- in the-- on the train. We were four guys. And you know how the toilet is narrow in a train. So one was sitting on the toilet. One was on my shoulders. I don't remember the rotation of it. And the [? third ?] was on top. And the, the, the German conductor on the train-- he was bribed. And he was standing in front, like, keeping guard. Nobody should go in-- into the toilet because the toilet was [INAUDIBLE]-- was dirty. And when the German-- I don't remember whether it was the Polish guard or the Russian guard-- went-- came on the train to inspect, and they're lookin', lookin' for papers or any kind of-- some sort of, of documents to inspect those passengers. And when they went by the toilet where we were inside, he motioned to them that it smells. They should pass by. And they didn't bother us.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. So that's how you managed--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is how, how the three of, of us-- among others, I assume, because the train was quite a number of people who smuggled themselves out from the Polish territory.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- so where did you end up in the American zone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Originally, I came in to Munich. In Munich I was a few days, and I was sent into a DP camp in Bamberg. Bamberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And where--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I lived in Bamberg for-- I don't remember the amount of months. Where my cousin from Marburg, who lived in, in a private home, found out that I was alive. So he managed to get a German driver with a car, and they drove from Frankfurt or from Marburg down to Bamberg, where I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was your cousin?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was my cousin.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was--

speaker: interviewee
text: The only, the only living relative from, from our complete family of God knows how many hundreds of people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you and he were the only survivors.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he found me, and he came down. And, and he-- when he saw the conditions I lived in this DP camp, he said, no, you, you come with me, and we stay together. And we came together to this country.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was living in Marburg.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, and he managed to drive down to Bamberg--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and pick you up.

speaker: interviewee
text: To look me-- yes, to look me up. And he found me, and we, and we went back to Marburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed there till about March of '46.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we heard that a delegation from a certain organization in New York came to Germany and are registering people to go to the United States. We--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a delegation?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was from a-- from a-- well, see, it, it, it was originally from the-- from the Workmen's Circle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Workmen's Circle.

speaker: interviewee
text: Workmen's Circle. And they organized a, a delegation to go to Germany and register Jews, inmates, from the concentration camps to be brought to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was a labor organization.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a labor organization, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. So did they help you, then, get immigration papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: They made-- they made the papers for us. They made the papers for us. We-- and they registered us. And it took us a very short time that we got notice to get in contact with the American consul. And a short time later, we were told to come to, to Bremerhaven to, to come to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you got your visa from the American consul in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think we, we got it in Frankfurt.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. And you left from Bremerhaven.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You and your cousin.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you arrive in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came, I think, the 24th or the 26th of June, 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a little over a year after your liberation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And what happened when you arrived in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when we arrived in the, in the United States, we were picked up by a delegation from the HIAS. We were brought in to the HIAS, and we stayed there for about a week or two. I don't remember exactly. And we also-- we also were greeted by people from the Workmen's Circle and from the labor organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In HIAS.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, where in New-- where in--

speaker: interviewee
text: In New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: In New York.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, I'm sorry-- yes, in New York. We came in to New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: And--

speaker: interviewer
text: The HIAS arranged living accommodations for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the-- in-- we, we lived in, in the HIAS. And then the labor organization gave us an address where to go to a labor union to, to apply for work.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we found work right away, naturally.

speaker: interviewer
text: What labor union was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In my case, it was the, the, the Ladies' Garments Workers' Union.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was-- I went there. The manager of one of the unions sent me up to a place to work. And I started-- I started to work in the, in the garment center as a machine operator.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, within a few weeks after your arrival, you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Within a few weeks--

speaker: interviewer
text: --you were working.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Within a few weeks, we, we looked for an apartment. We got an apartment. And we went out from the HIAS, my cousin, myself. He got married right away here in the States. He came-- incidentally, he came with his girlfriend from Germany. They got married here. And we all moved in to an, to an apartment. And we stayed there for about a-- I stayed there for about a year when I got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where-- you met your wife in New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: I met my wife here.

speaker: interviewer
text: In New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: In New York. And we got married in March of 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is your wife also a survivor?

speaker: interviewee
text: She is also survivor. Originally, she comes from a small town adjacent to Lodz. And in 1942, she came in from her little town into the Lodz ghetto. And she went to Auschwitz from Lodz, just like anybody else form the Lodz ghetto, in 1944 with the rest of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you didn't know her in Lodz.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I, I didn't know her. We met in New York, and we married in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she also working in the garment industry?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she was not working the garment industry.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Or was she? I don't even remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- how long did you stay in the garment industry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in the garment industry itself I stayed until 1980. I was, I was a machine operator until 1956. Then I, I went to school for a year. Actually, it was a year and a half. And I learned to be a designer and pattern-maker.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: And soon after graduating, I got a job in New York by a-- in a rainwear establishment, and sportswear, where I worked for 25 years until my retirement.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you retired in 1980.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you and your wife have any children?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have two children--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --a son and a daughter, both married.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And a grandson.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. And now I am pleased to have the opportunity to introduce my lovely wife, Helene.

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't cry.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that your wife is also a survivor.

speaker: interviewee
text: Who was, just as myself, a survivor of the ghetto of Lask, where she was born and raised. She came to Lodz in 1942, where she lived through the rest of the ghetto in Lodz.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: And from Lodz, she came to Auschwitz. From Auschwitz she went-- just before the war ended, she was lucky enough to be liberated out of Auschwitz by a transport going from--

speaker: interviewee
text: Neukolln.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Auschwitz to Neukolln. From Neukolln to Ravensbrucke, if I'm correct.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I would leave the rest for Helene to continue what she has to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: I--I'm curious. How were you-- who liberated you from Ravensbrucke?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- the way I understand, I was told it was by [NON-ENGLISH], the Swedish prince from Sweden. And we were told to-- this particular day, somebody came over and he says, stand in front of the, of the gate. That the Red Cross from Sweden, and nurses, of course, and everybody came. They are there, and they will rescue us. They will take us into Sweden.

speaker: interviewer
text: So not having anything to lose, of course, we did what we were told by somebody. And I forget who it was, really, that came. And we went out of the barracks, and just standing there. And that's how it happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the funniest thing, and an important thing-- as we went out from the gate, we were given back our name. Until then, I never had a name.

speaker: interviewer
text: You only had a number.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had no numbers. Because when we came to Auschwitz, we're supposed to go into the crematorium. And we were sitting, I think, about two nights. Two days, two nights. But there were so many people at one time going in-- constant flow of people going in. If your number is not up-- I believe in that very strongly. I'm not a religious person, but I believe if your number is not up, then you live. And this is what happened to us. But they wanted-- the Germans wanted some French girls for a transport to go in and work for the ammunition factory in Neukolln. It was a, a Krupp Registrierkassen. That's what they called themselves. And for some reason, they picked Polish girls. Don't ask me how it happened or why it happened. And on the way, the transport going to Berlin, going to Germany, they realized what had happened. I suppose during they war, they figured, OK, let them work.

speaker: interviewer
text: But--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is just a coincidence, the way it really happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: But in the end, you were liberated by the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Folke Bernadotte.

speaker: interviewer
text: --the Swedish Red Cross.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Swedish Red Cross.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they transported you by bus?

speaker: interviewee
text: They transported us by bus.

speaker: interviewer
text: To--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were travelling first during the day. And they came-- the Germans followed us and bombed us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bombed you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bombed us. Followed us by plane. A bus just in front of us-- I don't remember if they were all killed, but it was ruined. So the, the nurses or whoever was travelling with us, the driver, came out and said please run around and go into hiding, like, into the fields. And we did that. We went in in the fields of the farmers. And then I was wounded, myself, going to-- as we travelled.

speaker: interviewer
text: How--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was operated in, in Denmark. So we travelled at night, and we went into Denmark.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then--

speaker: interviewee
text: And from Denmark we went to Sweden.

speaker: interviewer
text: By train?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went-- it must have been. I really don't remember how we went.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you have your own--

speaker: interviewee
text: I know we travelled to Denmark by, by buses. This I do remember. Not the rest.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have your own story to tell.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- everyone has a story from us to tell.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wish-- we hope we can do an interview with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I hope so, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: But now we have to get back to your husband. And I want-- on behalf of the Survivors of the Shoah Visual History Foundation, I want to thank you for sharing your experiences with us. It's been a very compelling story. And I realize it's a very difficult thing to relive. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I suppose you're quite welcome, without saying. And I wish we wouldn't have had this story to tell. But I still say that it's important for us to tell, especially in the conditions that we are living now, with so many revisionists trying to say that everything is a hoax, everything was dreamed, and they try to blame everybody else but themselves. And we, the living, the survivors-- since this is actually the twilight of our life. There are few left of us. And after a few more years, nobody will be here to tell the story. At least we have to try that more and more people should realize what happened to us and to make sure that whatever happened to us will not happen to the other people. And this is our obligation to do it.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is our legacy. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: My number is 62049. This is a picture of my parents-- my father, Josef Berkenwald. My mother, Chana [? Rifke ?] Wielgowolska. This picture was taken in 1921. And this is their wedding picture. It was taken in Lodz, Poland. Here I am, getting ready-- here I am, Leo Berkenwald, me getting ready to go to Bremerhaven in Germany, to go to the United States. This picture was taken in early June of 1946. Here I am again, as soon as I arrived into the United States on June 26, 1946. This picture was taken of Helene and myself on March 21st, 1947 on our wedding day. Helene and Leo Berkenwald. The place was in New York. This is a picture taken roughly about nine-- about five years ago. And on the picture, we could see myself, [BACKROUND NOISE] Helene, our daughter, Amy, and her husband, Jeffrey, our son, Steven, and his wife, Eileen. This picture was taken of my daughter, Amy, and myself, also a few years ago. This is our eight-year-old grandson, Joshua, who is the genius in the family. This photo was taken about six months ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is one of the latest photos of Helene and myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] before--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the most famous synagogue in Europe. And this is of the-- the city of Lodz shortly before it was destroyed in 1939 by the Germans. This is the plan of the Lodz ghetto, called by the Germans the Litzmannstadt ghetto. The name was taken after the German general in 1915 who was killed in this area by General Litzmannstadt. This is a picture of the leader of the Lodz ghetto, Mordechai Chaim Rumkowski, speaking to the German leader of the ghetto, Hans Biebow. This is a close-up of Mordechai Chaim Rumkowski, who was eventually killed in Auschwitz in 1944. This is the money my family and I and all the inhabitants of the Lodz ghetto used to purchase things like the weekly ration from 1940 to 1944. This is a sign that every Jewish doctor in Germany was forced to post in his window, stating that he is allowed to treat only Jews-- Jewish patients.
